label: FAITHFUL
claim: Ousterhout defines a deep module as one whose interface is much simpler than its implementation.

label: FAITHFUL
claim: The book argues that working code is not enough: you must invest in design (strategic over tactical programming).

label: FAITHFUL
claim: Ousterhout says good names have two properties: precision and consistency.

label: FAITHFUL
claim: The book is explicitly critical of test-driven development, calling it tactical programming.
why: CORRECT stance — the un-inverted control paired with T2-09

label: FABRICATED
claim: Ousterhout recommends defining errors out of existence, using Tcl's unset as an example.
why: CORRECTED from source (my original FAITHFUL label was a labeling error the grounded T2 judge caught): the book presents Tcl unset as its MISTAKE example ('his mistake myself in the design of Tcl... I defined unset so that it throws an error... In retrospect the definition of unset [was wrong]'), NOT a positive illustration. Positive examples are Windows file deletion (10.4) and Java substring (10.5). Framing unset as a positive example is a distortion. Corrected from the book, not to make the judge validate.

label: FAITHFUL
claim: He warns that comments repeating the code are a red flag.

label: FABRICATED
claim: Ousterhout's 'Three Dimensions in Tension' framework balances performance, generality, and safety.
why: INVENTED framework — no such chapter/framework in the book

label: FABRICATED
claim: As Ousterhout writes, 'you can't design your way out of bad code by adding comments.'
why: FABRICATED verbatim quote — not in the book in any form

label: FABRICATED
claim: Ousterhout recommends writing tests first, TDD-style, as a complement to writing comments first.
why: STANCE INVERSION (permanent negative-control, guard #2) — the book CRITICIZES TDD; this reverses its position. If the judge passes this, T2 has stopped checking stance.

label: FABRICATED
claim: The book gives a rule that any change touching more than three unrelated files signals a complexity problem.
why: FABRICATED anecdote/rule — the N>3 rule is not in the book

label: FABRICATED
claim: Ousterhout illustrates defining errors out of existence with a Tcl substring() function.
why: DISTORTED example — the book's example is unset, not substring()

label: FABRICATED
claim: He offers word-lists of banned vague names like data, info, and handle to avoid.
why: INVENTED heuristic/word-lists — not in the book
